blocks.text: Every training block has a week that doesn't look like the others. For a lot of female athletes, that's their period — and how you handle it decides a lot more than four or five days of training.

Push straight through and you get a miserable, symptom-heavy session that teaches you training on your period is always bad. Skip the week entirely out of frustration or discomfort and you feel like you've broken your own consistency — which makes the week after harder to start too. Both are avoidable.
A period week doesn't need a different program. It needs a different lane inside the same one — lighter, safer, still moving. Here's how to run it.
This isn't about phasing your training

There's a lot of content out there about timing your training to the different phases of your cycle for extra gains — heavier here, more conditioning there. Ignore most of it. The evidence for a real performance edge from cycle-phased programming isn't strong enough to build a plan around, and chasing it adds complexity you don't need.

What actually matters is simpler: your period days are a symptom-management problem, not a performance strategy. The goal that week is protecting the habit of training — not manipulating the stimulus for extra gains.

items: Walking, cycling, or any steady cardio you tolerate — full duration, don't cut it short
Mobility and stretching — this is the week to do more of it, not less
Light hip and glute work — bridges, band walks, anything that isn't heavy

blocks.text: Movement isn't the enemy here. Gentle, rhythmic movement increases blood flow and eases cramping — the instinct to lie down and do nothing usually makes it last longer, not shorter.

items: Primary lifts — squats, deadlifts, presses — drop to about two sets, at a clearly lighter weight and RPE than normal. You're moving the pattern, not chasing a number
Anything with heavy ab-bracing or intra-abdominal pressure — weighted rollouts, planks, hollow holds — skip these outright for the week
Anything that leaves you doubled over finishing a set. If an exercise feels sharply worse than usual, regress it for the week rather than grinding it out

blocks.label: Rule
blocks.text: Full sets on cardio and mobility. About two easy sets on your main lifts. Zero on anything that loads the core hard. That's the whole adjustment — you don't need to redesign the week, you need to run this version of it.
Why this matters more than it looks

The physical adjustment is the easy part. The harder part is what happens in your head when the week doesn't look like your usual training — and that's usually where consistency actually breaks, not in the gym.

Athletes without a plan for this week tend to swing to one extreme or the other: force a normal session and feel worse for it, or cancel the whole week and feel guilty about it. Both come from not having a version of the week ready before it starts.

Cramping, fatigue, and a lower tolerance for load during your period are physiological, not a discipline problem. A quieter session isn't you being inconsistent — it's the correct response to what your body is doing that week. The inconsistency only shows up if you use the symptoms as a reason to disappear entirely.

blocks.label: Key
blocks.text: Consistency doesn't mean every session looks the same. It means you still show up, in whatever form the week calls for. A lighter, modified session that happens beats a normal session you skip out of guilt or discomfort.

blocks.label: Rule
blocks.text: Don't wait until you're mid-cramp to decide what today looks like — know the swap before the week starts: cardio and mobility stay full, lifts drop to light doubles, core-bracing work disappears. Decide it once, and every period week becomes a non-event instead of a crisis.
